ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper provides an empirical perspective on an already well-studied problem (loss surface geometry) by applying interpolation-based visualizations to compare a range of optimizers and showing optimizer-specific basin shapes, differing test-set disagreements, and the strong influence of batch normalization. These contributions are useful and provide new comparative observations (e.g., ADAM showing larger basins, BN simplifying surfaces), but they mainly extend existing visualization/analysis approaches rather than introducing fundamentally new theory or methods, so the novelty is moderate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main empirical claims are generally supported by the presented experiments: multiple optimizers were compared on multiple architectures/datasets (NIN/VGG on CIFAR-10, LSTM on PTB) using interpolated 1D/2D visualizations, training/validation metrics, and output disagreement measures, and hyperparameters were reported. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines and controls, but important caveats remain (low-dimensional linear/bilinear interpolations may not fully capture high-dimensional loss geometry, and the authors themselves note ambiguity between training plateaus vs true bad minima). Given these caveats and the lack of mentioned code/statistical testing, the work appears technically sound with minor limitations that do not overturn the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized (introduction → methodology → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early and clearly (different optimizers find qualitatively different minima; impact of batch normalization; visualization methodology). Notation and definitions are reported as consistent, and the use of 1D/2D linear, bilinear and barycentric interpolations provides effective, informative figures for the claims. Writing quality is described as good, with only minor presentation ambiguity in some limitations (e.g., uncertainty whether poor outcomes are plateaus versus bad minima) and no mention of released code, which are small issues rather than major clarity problems.

main_claims: Different changes to the optimization procedure (different algorithms or switching algorithms late in training) lead to different local minima of deep network loss surfaces.

Different optimization algorithms find qualitatively different types of local minima: the shape/size of the basin around minima is characteristic of the optimizer (e.g., ADAM-based methods exhibit larger basins than vanilla SGD).
Despite landing in different local minima, the different algorithms produce similar final training and generalization accuracy, though they make different errors on individual test examples.

Batch normalization materially affects the loss surface: with BN the quality of found minima is consistently good and surfaces are simpler, while without BN the quality depends strongly on initialization and surfaces are more complex.
_summary.methodology: Empirical visualization of low-dimensional projections of real loss surfaces (1D and 2D) via linear, bilinear and barycentric interpolation between weight vectors for NIN and VGG on CIFAR10 and an LSTM on Penn Treebank; compare optimization methods (SGD, SGDM, RMSprop, Adadelta, ADAM, and RK2-augmented variants) and measure loss along interpolations plus output disagreement between resulting networks.
metrics: average output L2 distance (and derived label disagreement %)
limitations: ADAM outperforms ADAM+RK2 (the RK2 augmentation did not improve ADAM in their experiments).
Without batch normalization, networks often fail to train from extreme initializations and the quality of critical points depends on initialization.
In some extreme-initialization cases it is unclear whether poor outcomes are due to training difficulty (plateau) or falling into bad local minima (explicitly stated uncertainty).
Runge-Kutta variants outperform SGD in many cases except the Midpoint method, but results vary by method and interaction with adaptive optimizers.
_summary.topic_area: Deep learning - loss surface geometry and optimization
